smartlead_campaign: OR #3 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
hey Leilani, noticed LPC Golf's founder never touched leather or a sewing machine before starting, curious how that came about.

on a separate note: I built 3 meta ad mockups from LPC's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook from your site and reviews.

 no pitch attached, it's already built.

 should I send it?

- Kyle

 P.S. you gotta show people what an amazing brand you got for real!!

thread.subject: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Leilani - the part most brands react to isn't even the ads, it's the brand playbook. it lays out your voice, the exact language your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's all set to send alongside the mockups. want me to send both over?

- Kyle

I am open to a conversation.  

 Best, 
Leilani

Love it - as promised in my first email, here are your 3 ad mockups built from LPC's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: RE: something for LPC
thread.body_text:
Hi Kyle,

I've booked some time on our calendar for Monday.

I had one quick piece of feedback on the mockups: the golf bag in image 2 appears way too small in comparison to the rest of the objects, but the others look great.

Also, just as a heads-up, our site is a custom Hydrogen build. I'm a software engineer, so I can implement changes myself, but please note that it is not a standard Shopify template.

Best,
Leilani

Leilani - fair call on the bag scale. the mockups were built from public site assets only, so think of them as angle concepts - with your actual product files they get a lot sharper. good problem to have.

 Hydrogen build is no issue at all, and having a software engineer on the brand side actually speeds everything up.

 talk Monday :)

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: LPC makes premium handmade leather golf headcovers in San Francisco, they run zero ads today and have almost no visible reviews, so this call is about starting clean from the 3 ads we sent, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A small craft brand called LPC Golf, legally Little Prince Customs, based in San Francisco. Headcovers are hand-stitched by an artisan named Karl. The site is a custom Hydrogen build and Leilani, the contact, is a software engineer who can make changes herself.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Handcrafted, customizable leather golf headcovers. They sell by collection: Quilted (20 designs), Exotic Classic (2 designs, embossed cowhide), and Artisan (8 designs, including rare materials). They also offer custom embroidery for initials, logos, or designs. We do not have their prices, so do not quote any.
deep_dive.positioning: The premium, heritage-craft end of golf accessories. Artisan and personalized, made to last, not mass-produced. They lean on premium leather, hand-stitching, and made in the USA.
deep_dive.who_buys: Golfers who care about craftsmanship and personal style, and will pay more for a handmade leather cover that stands out and lasts. Likely a gift buyer too, since custom embroidery suits gifting.
customer_voice: These headcovers are absolutely amazing! The craftsmanship is top-notch and I get compliments on them every time I'm on the course.
strengths: Clear, strong story: one artisan, hand-stitched, made in San Francisco. That is rare and easy to build ads around.
Customization and embroidery give a real reason to buy the premium version and open up gifting angles.
Software engineer on the brand side means site and tracking changes get done fast, no dev bottleneck.
gaps: Almost no social proof on the site. Only one visible testimonial and a self-claimed 5.0 rating with no volume behind it. That makes cold ads harder to convert.
No Meta ads found running, so there is no past data, no pixel history to lean on, and we are starting learning from zero.
No public catalog or prices. We cannot see which cover sells best or what an order is worth, so several plan numbers depend on answers from the call.

game_plan.headline: Start from the 3 ads we already sent, prove one hook can sell a headcover cold, then widen the winner and add embroidery as a gift play.

phases.goal: Prove that a cold stranger will buy one headcover from an ad at a price that works. Just find one winner.
campaigns: One sales campaign, small number of audiences, testing the 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet against each other.
A basic retargeting campaign for people who view the product but do not buy, once there is enough traffic to fill it.
lead_products: Lead with a Quilted collection cover. It is their deepest line at 20 designs, which usually signals it is what sells and what they can make consistently. The diamond-stitch look also photographs well and reads premium fast in a feed, which is what a cold ad needs. Note: confirm on the call which single cover is the actual bestseller before we lock the hero product.
phases.creative: Run the 3 ads we sent, but rebuild image 2 with real product files since Leilani flagged the golf bag scale. Lead with the hand-stitched, made in San Francisco angle since that is the strongest true claim they own. Do not use any banned claims (no 'best in the world', no 'lasts a lifetime', no fake scarcity).
phases.budget: Start small and steady. Goal is learning, not scale.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad clearly beats the others and produces sales at a cost the margin can carry. We know which hook and which cover to put money behind.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winner from month one and widen who sees it, without breaking what worked.
campaigns: Scale spend on the winning ad and cover in steps, not all at once.
Widen audiences: test lookalike audiences built off buyers and site visitors, plus broad targeting so Meta can find more buyers.
Strengthen retargeting now that there is real traffic, and add any customer photos or reviews we can collect.
lead_products: Keep pushing the month-one winner. Bring in one more Quilted design as a second creative so we do not burn out the single ad.
phases.creative: Make 2 to 3 fresh versions of the winning hook so it does not go stale. Start folding in real customer proof if we can gather any, since low social proof is a known weak spot.
phases.budget: Step spend up only while the return holds. Pull back fast if cost per sale climbs.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Sales hold or improve as spend goes up. We have a repeatable winner and a wider audience that still buys.

phases.goal: Scale the proven play and add a second product line to grow the order value.
campaigns: Launch a second angle around custom embroidery as a gift, aimed at gift buyers and timed toward the holiday season.
Test a 'build a set, save 10%' angle using their existing 4-plus discount to lift order value.
lead_products: Add the Exotic Classic embossed cowhide covers as the premium upsell, and push custom embroidery for gifting. These carry a higher-end story and a clear gift reason, which suits Q4 buyers.
phases.creative: Gift-focused ads for embroidery and the set discount. Keep the craft and made-in-SF story as the backbone.
phases.budget: Highest of the 90 days, but still only scaled on what proved out. Add budget for the gift push near the holidays.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The core cover keeps selling at scale, the set discount and embroidery lift average order value, and we head into the holidays with a working machine.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: About $50 to $75 a day to start
estimates.basis: Enough to gather a few hundred clicks in the first few weeks so we can tell which of the 3 ads actually works. For a premium, higher-priced item with no past ad data, this is the floor for learning without wasting money.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: Cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: We have no price, no average order value, and no past ad results. Once Leilani tells us the price of the lead cover and their margin, we can build a real target. Putting a number here now would be a guess and she would know it.

assumptions: Their headcover price and margin are high enough to carry paid ads. If margin is thin, the whole plan slows down.
The Quilted line is their bestseller. If a different cover sells best, we swap the hero product.
They can make and ship enough covers if demand rises, since these are handmade by one artisan. If Karl is the only maker, scale has a ceiling and month three changes.
Their tracking (pixel) is set up correctly, or Leilani can set it up fast. Without it we are flying blind.

questions.question: Have you ever run Meta ads before, even a boosted post? If so, what happened?
questions.listening_for: Any past spend and result. If they tried and it flopped, find out why. If they never have, we are truly starting from zero and expectations should be set that way.

questions.question: Who handles marketing today, and how are most of your sales coming in right now?
questions.listening_for: Whether they rely on word of mouth, Instagram, or a marketplace. Tells us how dependent they are on us and how much room there is to grow with ads.

questions.question: Roughly what does a headcover sell for, and is there room in the margin to pay for ads on top of the leather and the labor?
questions.listening_for: The price and whether the math can work. Handmade leather is costly to produce, so if margin is thin, ads may not pay. This decides if it is even a fit.

questions.question: Since these are handmade by Karl, how many can you make and ship in a week if orders jump?
questions.listening_for: The production ceiling. If one person makes everything, we cannot scale spend faster than they can build. This shapes month three.

questions.question: Which single headcover sells the most, and which do people ask about the most?
questions.listening_for: The real bestseller so we lead with the right product, not our guess of Quilted. If it differs, we swap the hero.

questions.question: When someone lands on the site but does not buy, do you know where they drop off?
questions.listening_for: Whether the problem is the ad, the price, or the checkout. If they have no idea, that is a tracking gap we fix first.

questions.question: You have great craftsmanship but very few reviews on the site. Do you have customer photos, testimonials, or messages we could use in ads?
questions.listening_for: Any proof we can pull in. Low social proof is a real weak spot for cold ads. Even a folder of texts and photos would help a lot.

questions.question: Is your tracking pixel installed and firing on purchases, or does that need setting up?
questions.listening_for: Whether Meta can actually see sales. On a custom Hydrogen build this is often not standard. Leilani can fix it, but we need to know before spending.

questions.question: Do people buy these as gifts, and does the custom embroidery get ordered much?
questions.listening_for: Whether the gift and embroidery angle in month three is real. If gifting is common, the Q4 push is a strong bet.

questions.question: Do you get much traffic from people searching Google for things like 'leather golf headcover' or 'custom golf headcover'?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is existing search demand. People do search for golf headcovers, so this is a real opening for Google later. If they already rank or get search traffic, that is a fast add-on.

questions.question: Are you showing up on Google when someone searches your brand name or product type today?
questions.listening_for: Whether the custom Hydrogen site is being found at all. A custom build can be strong or weak on search. This tells us if SEO is worth raising after ads are working.

objections.objection: We have never run ads and they seem expensive and risky.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small, around fifty to seventy-five dollars a day, and only to learn which of the three ads I already sent works. We do not scale until one proves it can sell to a stranger. You are not betting big on day one.

objections.objection: Our headcovers are premium and handmade. Will people really buy something this expensive from an ad?
objections.answer: Premium is an advantage in a feed, not a problem. People pay for craft when they can see it. That is why month one leads with your hand-stitched, made-in-San-Francisco story. We prove one buyer at your real price before we spend more.

objections.objection: Everything is handmade by one person. What if we get more orders than we can make?
objections.answer: Good problem, and we plan for it. We scale spend in steps tied to what Karl can build, never faster. If production is the ceiling, we hold spend there and push higher-priced covers and embroidery instead of just more volume.

objections.objection: We do not have many reviews to show.
objections.answer: I noticed that, and it is the one thing I would want to fix early. It does not stop us starting, but even a handful of customer photos and messages would make the ads convert better. Part of month one and two is collecting that proof and putting it to work.

red_flags.flag: Almost no visible social proof. One testimonial and a self-claimed 5.0 rating with no volume.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold buyers of an expensive handmade item want proof other people are happy. With little to show, ads convert harder and we may need to gather proof before scaling.

red_flags.flag: No Meta ads found running and no visible ad history.
red_flags.why_it_matters: There is no past data or pixel history to build on. Everything must be learned from scratch, so month one is about proving, not scaling. Set that expectation.
Production likely cannot scale as fast as ad spend. If we drive demand past what Karl can make, delivery times slip and returns or complaints rise. Scale must stay tied to capacity.

red_flags.flag: Custom Hydrogen build, not standard Shopify, and custom items are non-returnable.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Tracking may not be set up the standard way, so we must confirm the pixel fires on purchases before spending. And since custom orders cannot be returned, ads must set clear expectations to avoid unhappy buyers.

red_flags.flag: No public prices or average order value.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot confirm the margin carries paid ads or set an honest revenue target until Leilani gives us the numbers. This is the first thing to nail down on the call.

other_channel_openings: Google Search is a real opening. People actively search for terms like 'custom golf headcover' and 'leather golf headcover', so once Meta ads are proven we can catch that existing demand. Worth raising only after we confirm on the call that they get or want search traffic.
SEO is a maybe, not a yes yet. A custom Hydrogen site can rank well or poorly. Only pursue if the call shows they are hard to find on Google today and the demand is there.
